data.title: Britain heading for recession, Morgan Stanley warns
data.description:
The UK is heading for a recession if the energy price shock continues, according to a Wall Street bank.

Morgan Stanley said the Bank of England could raise interest rates if energy prices fail to come down, which would be likely to cause an economic downturn by the end of 2026.

Bruna Skarica, chief UK economist at Morgan Stanley, said Britain would face “a pronounced UK recession at the turn of the year” if energy prices stay at recent highs in the coming months and borrowing costs rise.

The war in Iran has caused oil and gas prices to soar in recent weeks, sparking fears over inflationary pressures and the impact on the UK economy.

The fallout from the Middle East conflict led to the Institute of Grocery Distribution warning on Wednesday that food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persists. 

That is more than double the current rate of 3.6pc.

Huw Pill, the chief economist at the Bank of England, said on Tuesday that “there are limits to what the MPC [Monetary Policy Committee] can do” to protect households from the energy price shock.

However, he stressed that Threadneedle Street “stands ready” to raise interest rates if the conflict in the Middle East drags on.

“The fog of uncertainty in which we always operate ​cannot be an excuse for inaction,” he said in a speech on Tuesday.

Mr Pill’s intervention comes as a growing number of economists warn that the energy crisis threatens to push the UK into a downturn.

Simon French, the chief economist at City stockbroker Panmure Liberum, said a recession in the final six months of the year was now “a real possibility”.

He added: “Events in the Middle East can, of course, reverse, but the UK economy’s soft underbelly has been revealed. It cannot be unseen.”

Thomas Pugh, the chief economist at RSM UK, a tax and consulting firm, said: “Everything depends on how energy prices move going forward, but we now expect growth of around 0.5pc this year with a decent chance of a recession.”

The war in Iran triggered the sharpest rise in factory costs since Black Wednesday in 1992, when Britain withdrew the pound from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism, triggering a huge sell-off of sterling that led to soaring interest rates.

The jump in factory input prices recorded by S&P Global in March underlines the effect of the Middle East conflict on manufacturers, which are heavily reliant on fuel and energy-intensive materials.

Both oil and gas prices have surged since the war broke out late last month, as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has strangled fuel supplies.

Echoes of Black Wednesday

Chris Williamson, of S&P Global, said: “Inflationary pressures have surged higher on the back of rising energy prices and fractured supply chains.

“The acceleration in cost growth in the manufacturing sector was especially severe, being the sharpest since the depreciation of sterling following Black Wednesday.”

The comparison with Black Wednesday will worry investors. At the time, then-chancellor Norman Lamont withdrew the currency from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism after failing to maintain the value of the pound.

The move led to sterling plunging by 25pc against the US dollar and 15pc against the German mark, with market panic prompting the Bank of England to raise interest rates from 10pc to 15pc.

Growth stalls as inflation rises

Alongside a rapid increase in manufacturing costs, Tuesday’s closely watched S&P survey also revealed that private sector activity in Britain fell to a six-month low with a score of 51, just above the 50-point mark indicating no growth.

Mr Williamson said the conflict in the Middle East had “hit the UK economy in March, stalling growth while driving inflation sharply higher”.

He added: “The full impact on inflation and economic growth depends not just on the duration of the war but also on the length of disruptions to energy markets and shipping.”

Meanwhile, in the US, business output slumped to an 11-month low in March, according to the S&P Global flash US purchasing managers’ index (PMI).

Companies raised their sale prices at the fastest pace since August 2022 and began cutting staff as they grappled with supply-chain delays and higher energy costs.

Mr Williamson said the data indicated that consumer price inflation will surge to 4pc, up from the current 2.8pc.

He said: “Companies are reporting a hit to demand from the additional uncertainty and cost of living impact generated by the conflict.

“Travel, transport and tourism related issues are compounded by financial market jitters and affordability constraints, notably including concern over the impact of higher interest rates, surging energy prices and supply-chain delays.”

The war has sent US borrowing costs soaring, with both two-year and ten-year treasury yields rising at their sharpest rate since October 2024 this month. 

Separate figures on Tuesday revealed that, in Britain, business optimism has tumbled to its lowest level since last June.

Jake Finney, a senior economist at PwC, said: “Private sector growth slowed considerably in March as events in the Middle East clouded the economic outlook. Demand has weakened, cost pressures have picked up, and the pace of job cuts has accelerated.”

Around a quarter of manufacturers said they had experienced longer delivery times from suppliers, causing some companies to build up stock in case of supply chain disruption.

Alongside cost pressures, businesses have warned that poor economic conditions weighed on retail sales in March.

Figures from the Confederation of British Industry showed that retail sales volumes fell at their fastest pace since the pandemic for the month so far, as cost of living fears caused shoppers to rein in spending.

It marks the fastest decline since April 2020, when many stores were closed during the first pandemic lockdown.

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”

data.title: BAE signs ‘landmark’ missile interception deal with the Pentagon
data.description:
Britain’s biggest defence company has signed a major deal with the Pentagon to supply missile defence equipment just days after Iran targeted the Diego Garcia base.

BAE Systems said the “landmark” seven-year deal would see it ramp up production of infrared parts used in defensive weapons, which could protect Britain and other US allies from ballistic missiles.

Michael Duffey, the US under secretary of defence, said the deal would help put the world’s “industrial base on a wartime footing” as the conflict continues to rage in the Middle East.

The agreement follows an attempted strike against the joint British-US Diego Garcia military base on the Chagos Islands. One missile was intercepted by a US warship and the second failed in flight.

It was the first confirmed use of long-range ballistic missiles by Iran and has raised questions about the readiness of Britain to defend against such attacks.

Military chiefs are said to have warned that Britain’s defence investment plan, which will set out the equipment the Government wants to buy, currently faces a £28bn shortfall.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, is said to be resisting calls from the Ministry of Defence for more money amid concerns that it would be wasted and require higher taxes.

One senior defence source with knowledge of the UK’s integrated air defence systems recently warned that Britain would struggle to fend off such a long-range bombardment.

BAE’s infrared seekers are used in America’s anti-ballistic missile defence system, known as “terminal high altitude area defence” or THAAD.

The FTSE 100 giant said on Wednesday it would increase production of the devices, which allow missile interceptors to spot incoming rockets.

Tom Arseneault, the president of BAE, said the deal with the Pentagon “provides a long-term demand signal that gives us the confidence to further invest in expanding our capacity and underscores the strength of industry’s collaboration with the department of war”.

Last summer, BAE delivered its 1,000th seeker to US defence giant Lockheed Martin, which makes the ballistic missile defense system under a pre-existing deal with the Pentagon.

Lockheed Martin has also announced it will quadruple THAAD interceptor production.

The Pentagon said the latest agreement provides BAE “with the stability required for long-term investment” in the components, which will form part of what Pete Hegseth, the US secretary of defence, has described as the “arsenal of freedom”.

Mr Duffey said: “Securing our supply chain is just as critical as our partnership with the prime contractors.

“This agreement with BAE Systems sends a clear, stable, long-term demand signal. We are providing the certainty our partners need to invest, expand and hire. This is how we place the industrial base on a wartime footing.”

data.title: Iran energy crisis ‘worse than 1970s and Ukraine war combined’
data.description:
The world is facing a worse energy shock than the twin crises of the 1970s and the turmoil that followed Russia’s invasion of Ukraine combined, the boss of the International Energy Agency (IEA) has warned.

Fatih Birol, the executive director of the IEA, said no country would be immune from the impact of the war on energy prices. He described the conflict in the Middle East as “very severe”.

Mr Birol said the IEA was consulting with governments in Europe and Asia on whether to release more of the world’s stockpiles of oil as the conflict entered its fourth week.

At least 40 energy assets had been “severely or very severely” damaged in the Middle East owing to the war, he added.

Donald Trump said over the weekend that he would “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if it did not fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the vital trade route, within 48 hours. The deadline is set to expire at close to midnight on Monday.

Iran responded to the US president by warning it would “irreversibly destroy” energy sites across the Middle East if he followed through on his threat.

Speaking in Australia late on Sunday night, Mr Birol said: “This crisis as things stand is now two oil crises and one gas crash put all together.

“The global economy is facing a major, major threat today and I very much hope that this issue will be resolved as soon as possible.

“No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction. So there is a need for global efforts.”

Mr Birol said the “most important solution” to the war was reopening the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway along Iran’s coastline where 20pc of global oil and gas exports pass.

The IEA said traffic through the strait had slowed to “a trickle” following attacks on vessels and energy infrastructure in the Middle East.

Mr Birol appeared to take a swipe at Mr Trump as the conflict showed no sign of ending.

“The ​depth of the problem was not well appreciated by the decision-makers around the world,” he said.

The price of oil was trading at around $113 a barrel on Monday morning and Mr Birol said there would not be a specific crude price that would trigger another release of global stockpiles.

IEA member nations agreed earlier this month to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from strategic stockpiles to ‌combat the spike in ‌global crude prices. ⁠The drawdown represented 20pc of overall stocks.

“A stock release will help to comfort the markets but this is not the solution,” said Mr Birol.

“It will only help to ⁠reduce the pain in the economy.”

He has begun a world tour in Canberra, saying the Asia-Pacific is at the forefront of the oil crisis given its reliance on the Strait of Hormuz for imports of oil and other crucial products such as fertiliser and helium.

After meeting Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, Mr Birol will travel to ‌Japan later this week before a G7 meeting.

data.title: Miliband’s clean energy tsar calls for more North Sea drilling
data.description:
The chairman of Ed Miliband’s state-backed energy company has argued for “more North Sea production” to slow job losses in the oil and gas sector.

Jürgen Maier, the former Siemens boss who now oversees Great British Energy (GB Energy), said more domestic oil and gas drilling was needed to buy time for workers in the North Sea so they could retrain for net zero jobs.

He said increased drilling would also generate higher tax revenues and produce fewer carbon emissions than imports.

Writing on LinkedIn, Mr Maier said that while he disagreed that higher North Sea production could cut energy prices, he supported the move to renewables “being an all energy and managed energy transition”.

The chairman’s comments will raise eyebrows at a time when critics of Labour’s energy policies say the Energy Secretary’s crackdown on the North Sea is accelerating the basin’s decline and causing thousands of job losses.

Within hours of his remarks being reported, Mr Maier clarified in another post that he was “fully supportive” of the Government’s position.

A spokesman for Mr Miliband declined to comment on whether any officials had contacted Mr Maier about his comments and referred The Telegraph to GB Energy.

A report by trade body Offshore Energy UK on Tuesday warned that “policy, not geology” was behind a collapse in investment.

It warned that on current trajectories, Britain will be reliant on liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipped from abroad for nearly half of its gas supplies by 2035.

By comparison, it argued, this could fall to less than 10pc if ministers reformed the windfall tax and granted new licences for dozens of projects.

The creation of GB Energy has been one of Mr Miliband’s flagship policies since returning to government in July 2024. Mr Maier was appointed as the company’s chairman that same month.

The debate around the North Sea has flared in recent weeks as the crisis in the Middle East has prompted global concern about oil and gas supplies and sent prices soaring.

On Tuesday, Mr Miliband repeated his insistence that drilling for more oil and gas in the North Sea would make “no difference” to production.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly argued that more drilling would be a waste of time because it would not bring down prices.

In a parliamentary question, Esther McVey, a Conservative MP, said sites were being closed down because of the Energy Secretary’s “Left-wing dogma”.

But Mr Miliband said: “We’re going to use the existing oil and gas fields for their lifetime. I think she’s referring to the question of exploration licences.

“What everybody says is exploration licences make no material difference to production levels and on the tax question.

“I hope she’ll carry on supporting the windfall tax and tell her front bench this would be the wrong time to abolish the windfall tax.”

data.title: Four simple steps to build a million-pound Isa
data.description:
As savings rates fall, and tax bills rise, it’s a key time to reassess your Isa strategy – doing it well could make you a millionaire.

At the last count, there were nearly 5,000 Isa millionaires in Britain, and the top 25 have pots averaging a whopping £11.3m.

But joining this exclusive club does not just happen overnight – it takes time and patience.

An Isa is an all-important tax shelter for your savings, where you will escape tax on dividends and capital gains tax on any profits when you sell, as well as income tax.

How much you invest, the investments you choose and their performance, are the main factors that will determine whether you can one day reach the £1m milestone.

Chancellor Rachel Reeves has confirmed reforms to cash Isas will be introduced in 2027 for savers under 65, but there is no suggestion (at the moment) that stocks and shares Isas are under threat.

If anything, the Government wants to encourage more small investors to put their money into the stock market, particularly into London-listed shares and infrastructure.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how to invest your way to £1m.

  • Could I become an Isa millionaire?
  • How to become an Isa millionaire in four simple steps
  • What does an Isa millionaire’s portfolio look like?
  • Best stocks and shares Isa providers

Could I become an Isa millionaire?

To get to £1m, investors will need to max out their Isa each year, and their investments must provide a certain level of returns.

Someone starting from scratch today putting the full £20,000 annual allowance into a high-risk stocks and shares Isa could expect to reach millionaires’ row in around 21 years, assuming 8pc annual returns, according to our calculator below.

If you’re more comfortable with lower-risk investments, investing at this same rate could still get you to £1m – but it would take around 35 years, assuming 2pc annual returns.

The average Isa millionaire has £1.35m, investment company Plum found. But the top 25 Isa investors are sitting on pots averaging £8.8m.

Lord John Lee became an Isa millionaire more than 20 years ago and has written for Telegraph Money sharing his insights.

Plum’s Rajan Lakhani said Isa millionaire wealth was continuing to grow “even as other savings and investment products lose a little of their shine due to rising complexity and lower interest rates.

“A large part of the Isa’s appeal is the flexibility and liquidity it offers investors. In simple terms, you can crystallise your wealth whenever you choose and regardless of age, unlike, for example, pension holdings or buy-to-let properties.”

It’s not just a feat for the elderly, either – at brokers AJ Bell, the youngest Isa millionaire is just 33 – and the oldest is 100!

How to become an Isa millionaire in four simple steps

There are four key habits that will help you become an Isa millionaire:

  1. Maximise your contributions
  2. Diversified investments
  3. Regular portfolio reviews
  4. Commitment to investing over the long-term

1. Maximise your contributions

To get to £1m as quickly as possible, the first step is to invest the maximum each year.

It is arguably easier to become an Isa millionaire today, with a £20,000 a year Isa allowance for savers (assuming you can afford to put away the maximum), compared to older investors who started out when Isas launched in 1999 with a £7,000 limit.

If you started saving today and the Isa limit remained at £20,000, it would take you 25 years to become an Isa millionaire, assuming an average annual return of 5pc.

The next key part of your strategy could be to invest early in the tax year. It means you will have up to an additional year invested, which will also help power portfolios in a rising market, as more of your assets are invested for longer.

2. Diversify investments

When it comes to selecting investments you’ll need a diverse, balanced mix according to your risk appetite. Buying individual shares can produce outstanding returns, but you are very exposed to a small number of companies (read on to discover the most popular shares held by Isa millionaires this year).

The alternative is investing in funds, either actively managed or “passive” where an algorithm mirrors a given index or industry. You might also wish to consider listed funds, also called investment trusts, which have been a good bet over the years.

At Interactive Investor, Britain’s second largest stockbroker, equities are the most popular type of holding among millionaires, accounting for 39pc of portfolios. Investment trusts come in a close second, at 34pc.

A total of 50 investment trusts would have made investors more than £1m if they had invested the full annual Isa allowance in the same company each year to 2024, according to research from the Association of Investment Companies, a trade body.

Investing the full Isa allowance each year from 1999 to 2024 – a total of £326,560 – and reinvesting the dividends into one of the four investment companies below would have generated a tax-free pot of over £2m at the end of January 2025.

These top four performing funds are: Allianz Technology Trust, HgCapital Trust, Polar Capital Technology and Scottish Mortgage. Among the common investment themes in these listed funds are technology and smaller companies.

While these figures are compelling, it is not advised to have all your money in one investment or one investment type.
The average Isa millionaire portfolio includes 23 holdings, according to AJ Bell.

3. Regular portfolio reviews

While choosing long-term investments is often a good strategy, it is still advisable to periodically assess and adjust your investment choices. As you approach retirement, your appetite for risk may decline.

Laura Suter, head of personal finance at AJ Bell, said: “Most Isa millionaires are in their 60s and 70s, illustrating the crucial impact of compound returns over the long-term. Nonetheless, nearly a fifth of Isa millionaires have hit the milestone before their 60th birthday.

“Astonishingly, a handful of extremely successful Isa investors in their 40s have racked up portfolios worth over £3m. Although it’s worth pointing out those with a sizeable portfolio at such a young age often tend to pursue a high conviction strategy focused on specific stocks, which won’t be for everyone.”

Ms Suter warned there was “no guaranteed recipe for success”.

“Some investors invest in highly diversified portfolios, while others have just a handful of positions. And while shares and trusts are especially popular among millionaires, there are plenty using funds too. The important thing is to invest in what you feel comfortable with and understand the level of risk you are taking in return for the potential reward.”

4. Commitment to investing over the long term

Investing over a long period is a tried and tested strategy.

The sooner you start saving the more you can put aside, and early contributions are the most valuable because they have the longest to grow.

Compounding will also boost returns. In simple terms, your money earns a return in the first year and both the original cash and the return benefit from any growth in the second year. In the third year your investment is further enhanced by any returns achieved. This snowball effect is known as compounding.

Experts insist that getting rich slowly is a smart strategy.

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, says: “Isa investors don’t take enormous risks.

“Their focus is to consistently invest as much as possible of their annual allowance, as early as possible in the tax year, in a diverse and balanced portfolio. And they’ve done this every year for decades.”

What does an Isa millionaire’s portfolio look like?

Listed investment funds have powered Isa millionaire portfolios at Interactive Investor, and account for the largest share of Isa portfolios.

Alliance Witan and Scottish Mortgage are the two most common investment trust stocks found in the average Isa millionaire top 10 holdings.

Investment trusts, the oldest form of collective investment in the UK, have several advantages over unlisted funds, according to advocates. One of these is their ability to hold back some of the income they receive from their underlying investments in good years to pay out to investors when times are tougher.

They can borrow money to invest extra than that provided by their investors to boost performance in an upturn. Many also have great track records for paying dividends. The downside is they can be expensive to trade, with many brokers charging flat fees for the purchase of shares, while units in unlisted funds can be purchased for a negligible fee.

Many investors have had success from unlisted funds, however. Popular funds include Rathbone Global Opportunities, Lindsell Train Global Equity, Fundsmith Equity and Fidelity Special Situations.

FTSE blue chips are widely held in Isa millionaire accounts. They include Shell and BP; Lloyds Banking Group and Aviva; GSK; and National Grid.

There is one passive fund in the top 10 at brokers Interactive Investor. These funds use computer algorithms to automatically track an index such as the FTSE 100. A common thread here is being – almost – fully invested.

Camilla Esmund, senior manager at Interactive Investor, said: “The not-so-secret sauce of Interactive Investor’s Isa millionaires is staying invested and being diversified – the latter involves spreading money across different asset classes, sectors, and geographies.

“Their success requires time, patience, and benefiting from the magic of compounding. Plus, our Isa millionaires are savvy to the fees they are paying, making sure they aren’t paying a percentage fee that risks eating into their growing pots.”

Best stocks and shares Isa providers

This is one of the most common questions we get asked on the Telegraph Money desk.

The truth is that the best stockbroker, fund supermarket or “platform” to hold your Isa investments depends on several factors: how much money is in your Isa, what support you need, and the types of investment you plan to make.

You pay holding fees to your provider, which can be a percentage or a flat charge.

On top of this you will pay fund management charges, which will vary greatly – you can find this information on the fund’s factsheet.

While a percentage charge can be cheaper for those with smaller holdings, it can quickly start eating into your returns as your portfolio grows. It’s a good idea to revisit your fees regularly and check whether you could get a better deal elsewhere.

Isa millionaire FAQs

If you’re looking for your investments to generate a regular income, in addition to growing to £1m, you might want to diversify your strategy to include dividend-paying stocks.

These companies pay out returns to investors, which you can either take to supplement your income or reinvest to hopefully speed up your portfolio’s growth.

The number of Isa millionaires is growing. This is to be expected; the longer investors have to grow their portfolios, the more millionaires we’d expect – plus, global stock markets have experienced a period of strong growth over the last couple of years, which has bolstered returns for many.

There could be up to 17,600 Isa millionaires by April, according to analysis of HMRC FOI data from Rathbones, a wealth management firm. As 5,070 people held Isa portfolios of £1m or more at the start of the 2023-24 ta year, this would mean numbers had trebled in just three years.

Yes, you don’t need to be an experienced investor to grow a seven-figure portfolio. It’s all about keeping consistent with your investing, and allowing enough time for your money to hopefully compound and grow over the years.

You might take an active role in choosing the stocks to invest in, or you might opt for a ready-made option where the hard work is all done for you.

Are you an Isa millionaire and happy to share the secrets of your success? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

data.title: Britain risks missing out on the great coal revival
data.description:
Coal, the dirtiest of the fossil fuels, has long been considered the bogeyman of the energy world. But the Iran war looks set to change all that.

The three-week-old conflict has damaged Qatar’s main gas plant and all but closed the Strait of Hormuz, locking up a fifth of the world’s gas supply and sending prices spiralling.

In response, countries with a heavy reliance on gas are reviving underused coal-fired power plants. To feed the generators, they are expanding mining or snapping up supplies from big producers such as Australia and Indonesia.

Coal, it seems, is back from the dead.

“In the Indo-Pacific region, but also in Europe for that matter, any utility or government that can switch from natural gas to coal-fired power generation will be doing that at the moment,” says Henning Gloystein of the Eurasia Group.

The Iran war may be over in weeks, as Donald Trump predicts, or it may drag on. But with Qatar saying that a full resumption of gas production could be years away, and the Strait of Hormuz revealed as an unreliable conduit, the shift to coal could outlast the current turmoil.

“I wouldn’t be surprised if coal has more of a renaissance in the following year, potentially longer,” says Caleb Jasso, of the US-based Institute for Energy Research.

The coal resurgence will be greatest in countries that can produce it domestically, such as China and India. But major Asian importers such as South Korea, Taiwan and Japan are switching from gas to coal.

Even in green-focused Europe, countries that still have coal capacity like Germany and Poland will follow suit.

There is no room on the bandwagon for Britain, though, which has gone further than most in shutting down coal-fired power.

The last coal-fired power plants closed in 2024, and the only debate is about whether to exploit the remaining oil and gas reserves in the North Sea.

“Countries like China, Japan, South Korea and Vietnam never switched coal off. They maintained multiple baseloads, so when gas tightens, coal capacity can step in,” says Michelle Manook, head of the global industry body FutureCoal.

She says European countries like Britain, which have pushed coal out, can now only toggle between gas and renewables. 

“If there is a lesson, it is this: don’t build your energy system on a single pathway, or you risk exposing it when it matters most,” Manook says. 

For many countries, gas was supposed to be the transition fuel that bridged the gap between their coal-dependent past and their renewables-based future. Gas emits less carbon dioxide and pollutants such as sulphur dioxide than coal, and is more efficient.

Demand for gas from Asia has doubled in the past two decades. Coal consumption has also grown, but has since tailed off as investment in gas and green energy has increased.

But almost no country yet has enough renewable energy, such as solar or wind, to power its economy nor the storage capacity to make renewables a reliable replacement for fossil fuels.

So for many countries – particularly those without a nuclear industry – a gas crisis can only push countries one way: back towards coal.

Iran ripped the gas market apart when it shut the Strait of Hormuz, and then used a bombing raid to cull almost one-fifth of Qatar’s production capacity for at least the next three years.

Even if the war blows over soon, Qatar’s damage will leave the gas market tighter than before. And the Asian countries that rely on Qatar can no longer trust the Strait of Hormuz.

Expectations of tight supply and a worldwide scramble for gas have doubled the price of liquefied natural gas (LNG) in Asia, taking it to a three-year high.

The response has been immediate: coal is back on the agenda.

Bangladesh, the Philippines, Thailand and Vietnam are among the countries looking to step up coal imports and boost coal-fired power generation.

Indonesia, a major coal exporter, has backtracked on plans to cut coal output this year and will now boost production. The government has also said, though, that it will prioritise domestic users over exports.

The economic powerhouses of Japan, Taiwan and South Korea, which still produce about a third of their energy from coal, are also following suit.

“We are seeing South Korea eliminating some of their coal capacity caps. We are seeing Taiwan look at restarting a mothballed coal power plant. We’re seeing strategic buying of coal stockpiles. They’re recognising the risk, and they’re hedging right now,” says Anthony Knutson, of analyst firm Wood Mackenzie.

Countries that make a lot of their own coal, such as China, India and Pakistan, are looking to boost output.

“In India and China, 85pc to 90pc of the coal is typically supplied domestically. So they are pivoting right now to incentivise more domestic coal production,” Knutson says.

Countries in Europe that still have the capacity to generate power from coal, such as Germany and Poland, will also feel tempted to pull this lever.

‘Coal has a bad rap’

But the European Union’s net zero targets create a strong political headwind.

Poland has cut coal’s share of electricity generation from 80pc in 2018 to 53pc last year, but the soaring cost of gas may stall this momentum.

Gloystein says the Poles will want to emulate China, where a transition to nuclear and green energy is backed up by coal as a safety valve.

“The calls to burn coal for longer will be very loud, because they have a big lignite industry that employs a lot of people and has strong unions,” he says.

In Germany, Jasso says the need for coal is more acute, given the reliance on imported gas and the lack of nuclear energy, but the political resistance is stronger.

“The biggest obstacle to getting back into coal is the rhetoric that has become so deeply set against it. Coal has a bad rap,” he says.

“I am fearful to see what pain point Europeans are going to have to endure in order to get to the real conversation of how do we become more energy self-reliant and diversify our imports.”

Gloystein reckons there is a majority in the German parliament to back a law that has already been drafted to bring back into service the 6.5 gigawatts of reserve coal capacity.

Yet if the country struggles to refill its gas reserves over the summer, the government may decide to bring the draft law to a vote, he says.

“And if gas prices remain high for another five or six years, the Germans might even start fiddling with their 2038 coal phase-out,” Gloystein adds. 

As for Britain, there is nothing left to switch on.

Manook says reembracing coal doesn’t have to be at odds with emissions-reduction targets if coal is made more efficient and its emissions are captured and stored.

“Across Asia, the focus has been on abating coal – improving efficiency and reducing emissions through advanced technologies,” she says.

But there are other drawbacks to coal. Its price tends to rise when gas prices rise and has jumped since the start of the Iran war.

Supply is unlikely to be directly disrupted by war, but major coal producers are regularly hit by cyclones, floods or earthquakes. There is also an ever-diminishing supply pipeline, as so few new mines are being commissioned.

The Iran war will also hit the cost of coal by pushing up the prices miners pay for diesel and for shipping their product.

“If you can’t produce coal domestically, you’re basically replacing one import risk with the other,” Gloystein says.

Kieran Tompkins, of Capital Economics, acknowledges that coal can increase diversity of supply for importing countries and self-reliance for producers. 

But its resurgence is really part of a bigger question: just how do they inoculate themselves against a chaotic and conflict-prone world?

“The legacy of the current crisis could be that energy security becomes an increasing priority,” he says.

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: BrewDog founder accuses new owner of wiping out small shareholders
data.description:
James Watt has blamed BrewDog’s new American owners for leaving thousands of early investors out of pocket.

The Scottish entrepreneur, who founded the craft beer business in 2007, said he was “disappointed” by Tilray Brands’ decision to wipe out BrewDog’s so-called “equity punks” in the wake of a rescue deal.

Mr Watt has criticised Irwin Simon, Tilray’s chief executive, after he failed to buy back BrewDog during an emergency sale process.

He had styled himself as a white knight to safeguard BrewDog’s army of smaller investors but was ejected from the auction at the eleventh hour.

This led to Tilray securing BrewDog’s brand, brewery and 11 of its pubs for just £33m this month.

TSG Consumer Partners, the private equity firm that bought into BrewDog in 2017, walked away from the sale empty-handed, as did Mr Watt and co-founder Martin Dickie. The equity punks’ investments were also wiped out.

Mr Watt said: “BrewDog was my whole life for almost two decades, and despite all the ups and downs, I am proud of what the team, in partnership with our community, was able to achieve.

“However, it is fair to say that it was certainly not the ending that I had hoped for.

“When Irwin Simon and I talked about the importance of the BrewDog community, he gave a clear indication that if he bought the business, he would ensure that equity punks retained an equity stake.

“I am really disappointed that he did not do this, and consequently, the Equity Punks were left with nothing. That is something I would never have done.

He also called on Tilray to reinstate the real living wage at BrewDog, which was controversially abandoned in 2024 as the company battled widening losses.

“For me, there is no BrewDog fightback without taking the community with us, without ensuring all team shares are honoured and without reinstating the real living wage,” said Mr Watt.

His comments come as Mr Simon sought to move on from Mr Watt’s past involvement in the business, which had previously resulted in claims of a toxic workplace culture.

Mr Simon told the Telegraph: “I haven’t spoken to James Watt, but listen, the past is the past.

“We don’t need James Watt, because what happens if James Watt were ever to come back here – and I’m being very clear, he’s not – then this becomes about James Watt’s second and third act.”

The £33m paid by Tilray was a staggering loss of value for a business formerly worth £1bn.

Following the deal, Mr Simon said there was “no money left” by the time he had taken over.

He said: “I’ve been involved in lots of acquisitions in my lifetime, and many in the UK, where you have time to go into data rooms, you have time to do the market studies.

“You have time to do the strategic plans and work out what you’re going to do with it. That was not the case here.”

BrewDog’s remaining 38 pubs were immediately closed in the wake of the bailout, leading to almost 500 job losses.

Tilray has since bought a further five BrewDog pubs in Britain, as well as its breweries in Australia and the US.

While Mr Watt was also wiped out as part of Tilray’s takeover, he has faced scrutiny over £100m he reportedly received as part of TSG’s investment in 2017.

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.

data.title: UK hits Chinese steel with 50pc tariffs to protect British industry
data.description:
Britain is preparing to hit foreign steel with tariffs of up to 50pc after warnings that a flood of cheap Chinese imports risked putting UK furnaces out of business.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed on Thursday that quotas for tariff-free imports will be cut by three fifths, with new duties placed on anything above this.

The protections echo similar crackdowns by the EU and the US, which have also moved to shield their domestic industries from cheap steel that is pouring in from China, Vietnam, Turkey and other countries with lower environmental standards.

Major producers, including Tata Steel UK, are understood to have warned that their businesses faced a potentially existential threat without urgent action.

Speaking to reporters in Port Talbot, South Wales – where Tata is building the world’s biggest electric arc furnace with government support – Mr Kyle said the combined moves would allow “the domestic market to flourish”.

He said: “This is what the sector is telling us that they need. This is the kind of investment that is aligned with where the steel sector itself, from across the breadth of the steel sector, is saying that it needs support.

“What you’re seeing today is a Government that matches the ambition that the steel sector has for itself.”

While the tariffs were welcomed by executives, the industry will be under pressure from the rising cost of natural gas, which is used by steel mills, and electricity as the Middle East crisis causes a price shock.

The new tariffs came as Mr Kyle unveiled a new national steel strategy. It sets an ambition for half of the steel used in the UK to be made in Britain.

The strategy says steel production is a vital capability needed for defence and critical infrastructure. Ministers intervened last year to take control of the last remaining blast furnaces operated by British Steel amid fears that the company’s Chinese owner, Jingye, was about to shut them down.

However, the Business Secretary stopped short of guaranteeing that government projects such as the Lower Thames Crossing would meet the threshold of using 50pc British steel.

Mr Kyle said the sector had been viewed as in decline for the last 50 years, adding: “I’m not here to either manage or mitigate de-industrialisation. I am here to re-industrialise. That is the purpose.

“Steel is going to be essential as we invest in our defence going forward, as we invest in our infrastructure and renew our infrastructure for a new generation, whether that be for trains, whether that be building out for the Oxford-Cambridge Arc and some of the new runways that we’ll be building, so there’ll be huge demand for steel on that quantity.”

The Government’s measures to protect domestic steelmakers replace older so-called safeguarding measures that executives had warned were out of date and were too generous to foreign producers.

Steel imports to the UK meet around 70pc of demand, up from 55pc in 2022. The biggest increases have come from China, India, Vietnam, South Korea, Turkey and Algeria, according to trade body UK Steel.

The global steel market is experiencing a glut because of overproduction by China and other Asian countries.

According to the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), world capacity for steelmaking is now about 2.55 billion metric tonnes per year. That is about 680 million metric tonnes more than there is demand for.

British producers have also been hurt by rising energy costs, although various government schemes to subsidise their power costs are now in place and are expected to be expanded.

Tata Steel UK’s new electric arc furnace will benefit from this support when it begins operation in late 2027.

The site’s power requirements are so large that the National Grid is building a new substation in Port Talbot to accommodate its needs.

It is poised to take around 70,000 tonnes of scrap metal per week and produce more than three million tonnes of steel a year, making it the biggest electric arc furnace of its kind in the world.

‘Doubling down’ on British steel

Rajesh Nair, the chief executive of Tata Steel UK, said the Government’s tariff changes would ensure Port Talbot is on a “level playing field” with foreign steelmakers.

However, he also warned that the domestic steel industry would thrive only with higher demand from the construction industry.

Mr Nair said: “When there is manufacturing, when there’s growth, steel grows.

“The UK has one of the highest intensities of steel in construction, probably globally. But the reason why it’s still not going is because the construction industry is declining, so you have got to figure out ways of improving demand.

“If you increase construction, you push consumption.”

Asked whether government infrastructure projects such as the Lower Thames Crossing would use at least 50pc British steel, Mr Kyle replied: “We have a commitment to use British supply wherever possible, and wherever practicable, and we’re going to be doubling down on that.

“So of course, wherever there’s opportunity, we’ll be exploring it.”

According to internal assessments for National Highways, which is running the project, 85-90pc of steel for the Lower Thames Crossing could be sourced domestically.

However, there have been warnings that foreign steel could be used instead because it is cheaper.

data.title: Savannah Guthrie: I wake up imagining my missing mother’s terror
data.description:
Savannah Guthrie has said she wakes up every night imagining the “terror” her mother must have gone through during her suspected kidnapping.

The US morning news anchor made the comments in her first interview since her mother, Nancy Guthrie, went missing at the end of January.

The presenter struggled to hold back tears as she told her NBC Today co-host Hoda Kotb: “To think of what she went through. I wake up every night in the middle of the night.

“And in the darkness, I imagine her terror. And it is unthinkable, but those thoughts demand to be thought.”

She added that her family was “in agony” over the disappearance of the 84-year-old.

Mrs Guthrie was last seen at 9.45 pm on Jan 31, when her family dropped her off at her home just outside of Tucson, Arizona. She was reported missing the following day.

The case has puzzled investigators, who have turned up few clues about her whereabouts.

Ten days into the search, authorities released a video of a masked, armed figure who appeared to tamper with a security camera at Nancy’s home in the early hours of Feb 1. But law enforcement has not yet been able to identify them.

Drops of Mrs Guthrie’s blood were found on her front porch. Her phone, Apple Watch and vital medications were also still inside the home.

Chris Nanos, the Pima County sheriff who is overseeing the investigation, said DNA from an unknown source had also been found.

Ransom notes sent to media outlets in the days after her disappearance appeared to chart a possible path to Nancy’s safe return.

Savannah Guthrie posted a video of herself and her siblings to her Instagram account and addressed the possible kidnappers directly. They were “ready to talk,” she said. But their calls appear to have gone unanswered.

The Guthrie family has since offered a $1m (£747,000) reward for Nancy’s recovery.

“Someone needs to do the right thing. We are in agony. We are in agony. It is unbearable,” Savannah said in a clip of the interview with Ms Kotb broadcast on Wednesday.

NBC will air more of the interview on Thursday and Friday.

data.title: British factory lines face shutdown within weeks
data.description:
British factories face production cuts within weeks because of surging gas prices, industry bodies have warned.

Ceramics businesses, glass manufacturers and chemical companies could bear the brunt of the global energy crisis because of a heavy reliance on gas, which has soared after attacks on Gulf energy facilities.

These sectors require large amounts of gas to power the high-temperature machines required to manufacture their goods – leaving them more vulnerable to the price spike.

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, said: “There’s a high risk that those factories exposed to the spot market prices will cut production in the next few weeks.

“We’ve seen a series of closures, particularly on the chemical side and ceramic side, and that was even before gas prices escalated a few weeks ago.”

Gas prices jumped by 11pc on Thursday after Iran carried out missile attacks on energy sites in Qatar and Saudi Arabia. European benchmark gas prices climbed to €60.72 per megawatt hour – their highest level since January 2023.

Mr Geveke warned that if energy prices remain at their recent highs the Government would need to introduce support within “weeks” or even sooner.

He added that ministers should prepare to intervene “in case this situation prolongs and prices increase further”.

Production cuts in British factories would have wider consequences for many supply chains, triggering knock-on effects across the economy.

The latest escalation in the Iran war adds to fears that companies already grappling with high taxes and levies could be put out of business by soaring energy costs.

Natural gas prices, a key input for UK electricity prices, have jumped by more than 90pc since the war in Iran began.

Frank Aaskov, from UK Steel, said some members had warned they would have to curtail production in the next few weeks.

‘Uncompetitive electricity prices’

He said that while the UK’s “uncompetitive industrial electricity prices have long been the primary barrier to the steel industry’s ability to compete internationally”, the situation was being exacerbated by escalating conflict in the Middle East.

The warnings add to the mounting pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to unveil a package of support for companies grappling with a sharp increase in energy bills.

Nadine Bloxsome, the chief executive of the Aluminium Federation, said that unless the Government rolled out financial support measures there was a “real risk that UK manufacturing will become less competitive”.

She said: “UK aluminium manufacturers are already operating under significantly higher energy costs than many of our global competitors, and further increases driven by geopolitical instability only intensify that pressure.”

Any government intervention on energy price for businesses would be expensive. While gas prices are nowhere near the levels seen shortly after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, energy subsidies for businesses cost taxpayers an estimated £18.4bn in 2022.

Tom Jones, from the British Allied Trades Federation, said that the recent energy price spikes are “very destructive” for companies.

He added: “It seems that private industry, particularly manufacturing, takes the brunt of whatever comes along.”

The escalating attacks on energy facilities across the Gulf have led to fears that Britain will face oil and gas shortages in the coming months.

Mr Jones said members were concerned about the “impact of rationing that may come in if we can’t get enough gas or enough oil”.

A Government spokesman said: “We know this is a tough time for our energy intensive industries, who are grappling with increased fossil fuel prices. The best way to tackle this is getting onto home-grown power which we control to bring down bills for good.

“We have published a new steel strategy today that will boost advanced manufacturing across our economy, and we are working with all sectors to understand the impact of the situation in the Middle East and explore potential solutions.”

data.title: £50bn construction giant abandons London stock market
data.description:
Building materials giant CRH is to quit the London Stock Exchange (LSE) in a further blow to Britain’s standing as a global financial hub.

CRH, which is worth nearly £54bn, said it had decided to exit the UK market because it was hamstrung by high regulatory costs and low levels of trading activity.

The firm’s shares will now be traded only in New York, where they had been listed in addition to London since September 2023.

The move serves as yet another setback for London’s struggling stock market, with CRH joining a string of high-profile companies that have defected to New York in search of higher valuations.

Online payments company Wise, worth £12bn, last year transferred its main share listing to the US, saying the move would allow better access to the world’s most liquid stock market.

Meanwhile, AstraZeneca said in September that it would upgrade its presence on the New York exchange in what bosses described as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

While the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain based in the UK, the announcement has stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker and most valuable business could ultimately use the move as a stepping stone to quitting the LSE.

CRH had already declared New York as its primary listing and had been removed from the FTSE 100 index.

The company, which is based in Dublin, said a review launched last month had concluded that quitting London completely was “in the best interests of CRH and its shareholders”.

It said: “CRH carefully considered, among other factors, the level of trading activity for its ordinary shares on the LSE as well as the additional cost, regulatory and administrative obligations arising from retaining the LSE listings.”

The firm said it had asked the Financial Conduct Authority to cancel its London listing, with the move expected to be completed by Apr 20.

CRH said on switching to the dual listing three years ago that the decision had also been partly driven by a US push for onshoring manufacturing activity, something that has gathered pace under Donald Trump.

CRH, which was founded in 1970 via a merger of two Irish cement and quarrying companies, already counts North America as its biggest market.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The BBC stares into the abyss
data.description:
As its new director-general, Matt Brittin, prepares to take the reins, the response by the BBC to the Government Green Paper on renewal of its Charter is a cry of anguish. A mixture of self-aggrandisement and self-pity, it demonstrates a remarkable lack of self-awareness. The document is full of information, but is unreliable. It argues for “real reform” of the Corporation’s financing, yet its bedrock demand remains that everyone must be forced to pay for the BBC, whether they like it or not.

The central thrust of the document is that 94 per cent of households use BBC services, but fewer than 80 per cent pay for them. Given that in 2025, 86.5 per cent of households still held TV licences, the recent decline in numbers is precipitate, even allowing for 700,000 free licences (which the BBC provides for those people over 75 in receipt of pension credit).

Whatever the figure, the direction – and speed – of travel is unmistakeable, and threatens the BBC with irreversible decline. Much of the document is devoted to plaintive claims that the BBC is heroically cutting internal costs, to the tune of £2bn during the current Charter period. Yet these “savings” are entirely nominal: every pound saved in one area is spent in another, with cheaper programmes replacing more expensive ones. 

The document’s claim that the BBC invests £3bn in content a year is highly questionable. As the Green Paper itself made clear, the BBC spent £1.2bn, or barely 30 per cent of its TV licence fee income, on original UK content in 2023. The document boasts of a reduction in headcount of 2,000 in recent years: yet last year the BBC cut just 22 from a payroll of 21,795.

Equally questionable are the proposed solutions to the rapid decline of the licence fee. The BBC concedes that the threat of prosecuting evaders has lost most of its impact, with a reduction of 80 per cent in court cases brought each year since the pandemic.

For the BBC, any reform of its funding must be “universal”, “fair” and “sustainable”. Above all, it must be inherently unavoidable.

Taxation has been ruled out as a source of funding by the Government, and with at least 10 million people not paying tax, such a plan would anyway fail the BBC’s fairness criterion – too many free-riders. Advertising as a full replacement for the licence fee is also deemed non-viable: it would risk bankrupting ITV and Channel Four.

The BBC document floats the idea of some kind of levy, perhaps on broadband fees. But why would the highly competitive broadband market put up with a requirement to collect money on behalf of the BBC?

Another suggested option – a council tax surcharge – makes little sense when council tax arrears are already higher than the level of licence-fee non-payment. Again, why should councils collect money for the BBC when they are themselves cash-strapped? An extra charge would surely increase arrears. The likelihood is that the BBC would not only lose control of its income, but have no idea what its revenue would be.

Then there is the notion of changing the law and forcing subscribers to Netflix and other streamers to pay the TV licence. But how would that work? Most streamer subscribers already have a TV licence – would the companies be compelled to hand over their customer details? How could that not be a breach of data protection rules?

The BBC resents the streamers, claiming that they are a threat to the UK audio-visual sector (even though they spend more on UK content than the BBC), and that their global reach gives them too much commissioning power.

But a BBC primarily funded by subscription could replicate those business models, exporting its streaming services rather than just individual programmes (a trade of which it rightly boasts). The BBC already runs a subscription service in the US called BritBox. Indeed, it owns a channel business in the UK – UKTV – that earns hundreds of millions in subscription and advertising revenue. That is never mentioned in the document.

The BBC’s stated objection to subscription is that it excludes certain households (which either choose not to subscribe, or find the cost too high), and that the resultant service then becomes too narrow (that is, not “universal”), “prioritising the tastes of those left paying”. This is, of course, nonsense.

The BBC has forgotten its own history. For more than three decades, it was a subscription service, both in radio and television, with customers choosing to acquire the necessary reception equipment and pay the relevant licence fee. It was not “universal”, not least because its transmitters could not reach everyone.

Did the BBC “narrow” its output, to serve its existing subscribers (what would that have involved, anyway)? Of course not – like any business funded by paying customers, it strove to widen its appeal, and attract new subscribers: just like Netflix and the other streamers, to whose services some 80 per cent of UK households currently subscribe, entirely voluntarily.

The irony is that the BBC was there right at the start of streaming, with its iPlayer technology, launched in the same year as Netflix. It is a very popular service. Indeed, two-thirds of viewing of all BBC drama is nowadays through downloads, rather than live transmission. Frustratingly, the BBC spends hundreds of millions a year on dual transmission systems, because it cannot bring itself to adopt encryption and subscription, and abandon terrestrial distribution – which is anyway rapidly approaching obsolescence.

The document states an ambition for the BBC to become “more directly accountable to audiences”, whatever that means. Yet the most obvious form of accountability – the right not to pay – is denied. Choice is not a word to be found anywhere in the document.

The BBC asks to escape the constraints of regular reviews of the Charter, and ministerial appointment to its board of directors. Yet as long as the BBC is funded by a mechanism underpinned by legislation, with the level of charge set by Government, how can the BBC expect to be cut free?

Until the BBC itself walks away from the licence fee, and abandons the shibboleth of “universality”, it will continue its steady decline – as the document puts it – to eventual irrelevance. The new director-general about to start work at the BBC has a background in technology, not programme-making. 

He has had no input into this blinkered document. His appointment gives the BBC a chance to make a clean break with its outdated dogmas and finally face up to the reality that confronts it. Experience, sadly, suggests we do not hold our breath.

David Elstein has been a BBC producer, director of programmes at Thames Television and chief executive of Channel 5
