data.title: Five changes every landlord must prepare for in 2026
data.description:
Britain’s landlords must brace for a year of new rules, as the Government plans to enforce a raft of regulations and additional tax requirements on a sector already burdened by rising costs and tighter rules.

While the Government insists the changes are about raising standards and strengthening protection for tenants, landlords warn that it could lead to worse outcomes for renters if enough landlords decide to leave the market entirely.

Here, Telegraph Money sets out what’s coming for landlords this year and what you need to do to get prepared.

Making Tax Digital

When? From April 6.

What does it mean for landlords?

Making Tax Digital (MTD) will require certain taxpayers to report their income and expenses to HMRC four times a year, in addition to annual tax returns. This doesn’t only affect landlords – other taxpayers, such as the self-employed, are also affected.

For landlords specifically, MTD applies to those whose qualifying income exceeds £50,000, but this income doesn’t have to be from property. For example, if you have income from property and self-employment that is more than £50,000 combined, then you would need to register.

Those required to take action should have already received a letter from HMRC, but, as always, the onus is on you to check whether you should be signed up.

Heather Powell, of accountancy firm Blick Rothenberg, said: “The first return, reporting rental income and expenses for the three months from April 6, 2026 to July 5, 2026 has to be filed by Aug 7, 2026, with filing from ‘compliant software’ or bridging software that picks up the financial information from an Excel spreadsheet.”

As a landlord, you need to be on your game, as there is an initial £200 fine after two late filings.

While HMRC says this new filing regime will “increase the accuracy of filings”, the shift will undoubtedly feel daunting for the many landlords juggling multiple properties and income streams.

What’s more, the submissions must be made using MTD-compatible software. There is a long list of companies offering options, many of which require payment. Whether landlords try to grapple with this on their own or with the help of an accountant, they’re likely to incur additional costs.

The key right now is to find out whether you’re affected and, if you are, get your accounting records in order and start moving towards digital record-keeping.

Renters’ Rights Act reforms come into force

When? May 1.

What does it mean for landlords?

After becoming law in November, the first phase of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) 2025 reforms will come into effect from the start of May – and there’s a lot to get your head around.

Michael Dear, landlord insurance specialist at insurer Hiscox, said: “The RRA brings changes such as limits on rent increases and the end of fixed-term tenancies, which aim to make renting fairer.”

In addition, there are new rules on “no-fault” evictions and tenant requests to keep pets.

With only a few months to go, awareness is believed to be relatively high among landlords, but concerns remain that actual understanding is patchy.

Sián Hemming-Metcalfe, operations director at Inventory Base, a supplier, said: “Most landlords know the RRA is coming, but far fewer have grasped what it means in practice. This is not a cosmetic change; it rewrites how possession works, how rent is handled, how tenancies are structured and what landlords will need to evidence if something goes wrong.”

As such, there are a few things you should prepare now, if you haven’t already.

  • Before the abolition of Section 21: Review the new rules in detail, update your tenancy documentation, and potentially seek legal advice if you are likely to need to evict tenants.
  • Prepare for the end of fixed-term tenancies: All existing and new fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies must convert into rolling tenancies, so think now about how you need to change your existing processes and update your contract paperwork.
  • Prepare for rent increases being restricted to once per year: Now is a good time to assess how much rent you’re charging. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, said: “With rent increases due to be open to tribunal challenge from May, many landlords are using the months ahead to ensure their rents are aligned as closely as possible to market levels.”
  • Plan for new pet rules: Requests to keep pets cannot be turned down “unreasonably” starting in May. Before this, it’s prudent to consider any practical adjustments that might better protect your property from pet damage, update your inventories and perhaps your tenancy agreement paperwork and insurance policy to make sure you’re covered.

For more information on what the new law means for landlords, see our full guide to the RRA.

Provide information to existing tenants

When? By May 31 to avoid penalties.

What does it mean for landlords?

This is part of the RRA, but has its own separate deadline, so we thought it was worth pointing out separately.

The requirement is for landlords to issue tenants with key information in writing, setting out the details of the tenancy, including – but not restricted to:

  • The property address
  • The tenancy start date
  • The rent amount and when it is due
  • A statement to confirm that any proposed rent rises will be in accordance with section 13 of the Housing Act 1988
  • Details of the tenancy deposit
  • Requirements should apply if either party wishes to end the tenancy.

New tenancies made on or after May 1 must include this information as standard. If you have existing tenancies – ones already in place on May 1 – you will need to issue this information to them if you haven’t already.

If it’s already part of their tenancy agreement, you must instead give your tenants a government-produced information sheet telling them how changes being made by the RRA might affect the tenancy. This is expected to be published this month and can be sent as a hard or digital copy.

Don’t ignore this, as failure to comply will be considered a breach of the act and can mean hefty penalties, starting from £4,000.

You can read the full government guidance for landlords here.

Further ahead: New Energy Performance Certificate metrics

When? Had been planned for the end of 2026, but recently delayed to 2027.

What does it mean for landlords?

Under current plans, landlords must ensure that all rental properties have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of “C” or above by 2030 or spend £10,000 to try.

However, the existing EPC system is also due for reform, with the single metric to be replaced by four new metrics, which had been expected in the latter half of this year. The Government has since announced the new system will not be introduced until 2027. 

Once introduced, landlords will have to achieve a rating of “C” in the property’s “fabric performance” (which considers insulation) and least two of the other three new green metrics.

The aim is to help landlords (as well as other homeowners and renters) understand certain headline measures in their properties. The assessments will also cover the heating system (encouraging the adoption of heat pumps), the building’s “smart-readiness” (the potential to integrate smart technology) and practical steps to improve homes and reduce energy costs.

The idea is that this will provide a broader picture of a property’s energy profile.

The Government estimates that the average landlord will need to spend £5,400 to meet the standard, so if you know your rental property or properties have a poor EPC rating, it’s worth planning now.

You may need to start setting money aside to fund improvements and start looking into grants you can apply for (such as the Boiler Upgrade Scheme if you are considering a heat pump).

Jason Harris-Cohen, managing director at LandlordBuyer, said: “This represents a significant retrofit challenge for landlords, many of whom face difficult decisions between absorbing costly upgrade expenses, raising rents, or exiting the market altogether.”

Minimum qualifications for letting agents

When? TBC.

What does it mean for landlords?

Last year, the Government set out proposals to introduce mandatory qualifications for letting agents to raise professional standards across the sector.

The plans include a new code of practice establishing minimum requirements for all residential property agents, including estate agents, managing agents and letting agents.

As yet, no date has been confirmed. However, with many landlords reliant on agents to meet increasingly complex obligations, there is growing pressure for clarity on when the reforms will take effect.

Until this happens, any landlords using letting agents to manage their properties should ensure that the agents are trained and prepared for the forthcoming law changes. For instance, is there a named compliance specialist available to answer questions about the new rules? Are there facilities for staff to be trained on the changes and any updates? Are they updating their services and terms in accordance with the new rules?

If not, you might want to consider moving to a different company, as a letting agent’s failure to follow the new law will embroil you as the landlord.

data.title: Why rip-off fees could not save Britain’s biggest car park owner
data.description:
Sympathy was in short supply when NCP called in the undertakers this week.

Despite the threat of nearly 700 job losses, the collapse of a company with a reputation for routinely ripping off drivers was widely celebrated.

Within hours of NCP’s Japanese owners calling in administrators at PwC, motorists were rejoicing on internet message boards and social media channels.

“Haha!!!! That’s what happens when you charge £30 for 2 hours parking,” said one Facebook user.

Some expressed shock at how a business renowned for its high prices could come unstuck.

“I’ve no idea how they can be broke with the rip-off ticket prices,” said one.

Others pointed out that those same high prices may be precisely the reason why it had fallen into administration. In central London, NCP charges as much as £60 a day for parking.

“To be avoided if possible, which is probably why they have gone bust!” another joked.

The shocking reality is that despite charging extortionate sums to use one of its car parks, NCP had not been profitable for years – the result mainly of it being treated as a get-rich-quick scheme by a succession of private equity firms over the last 25 years.

Filings submitted to the High Court on Monday fail to address whether NCP had effectively priced itself out of existence. Nor do the documents discuss the recklessness of past owners.

Backers Tokyo-based Park24 blamed a combination of the pandemic and Vladimir Putin.

Yet the ghost of the extreme financial engineering that had saddled NCP with increasingly punishing costs it could no longer meet had not been expunged entirely.

“Persistently high inflation” had led to “rising inflation‑linked rent payment obligations,” Park24 said.

PwC added: “The high concentration of long-term, inflexible leases has meant the company has been unable to reduce costs…resulting in ongoing trading losses.”

Incredibly, the issue is a hangover from an agreement struck in 2003 shortly after the company fell into the hands of buyout fund Cinven. Founded in 1977 from the British Coal pension scheme, Cinven was the first of a series of private equity owners that would go on to treat NCP as little more than a giant cash cow.

Just 18 months after the firm had snatched the car parks giant from under the nose of rival venture capitalist Apax Partners, Cinven subjected the business to an aggressive sale-and-leaseback deal in which a big chunk of NCP’s property assets were separated from its operating arm.

The ploy was a classic financial engineering tactic of the pre-financial crisis private equity boom in which hundreds of deals were financed by selling off the assets of the companies that had been acquired. The returns were generally used to quickly pay down debt or to fund large dividend payouts to investors.

One hundred NCP car parks were packaged up and sold to an investment vehicle backed by the Royal Bank of Scotland. The move raised £600m, enabling Cinven to repay most of the £700m it had borrowed from RBS to bankroll the £820m it paid for NCP the previous year.

In 2005 the firm offloaded the company to rival private equity house 3i for £550m, netting three times its money. Cinven boasted that the break-up of NCP had enabled it to “return 70pc of the original investment to investors ahead of the sale”.

What executives were less forthcoming about was that the sale-and-leaseback had also left the business stuck with a high rent burden. At the time, the rent on those 100 car parks was £41.5m a year, and the leases on them did not run out until 2037. It was a form of financial purgatory that the company made several failed attempts to escape from in the intervening years.

Yet in many ways, Cinven was merely copying the blueprint laid out by NCP’s previous backers. The business was bought by US tourism giant Cendant in 1998, and within a year it had offloaded NCP’s Green Flag roadside recovery unit for £250m. Cendant reportedly went on to extract more than £200m from the business in the form of dividends and property sales.

NCP’s sale to 3i saw the company hollowed out once more. Barely 18 months after buying the business, it was split up again, with 3i selling its car parks to Australian investment house Macquarie for £790m.

The firm banked a £235m profit – equivalent to three times its investment – and held onto the part of NCP that ran parking enforcement services on behalf of local authorities. In 2010, that business was sold for £120m.

Weighed down by £500m of fresh borrowings that Macquarie took out to finance its takeover and a pile of onerous rental contracts on nearly 130 car parks, NCP was eventually forced to beg its banks and landlords for a bailout.

A messy financial restructuring was hammered out in 2012 in which its main lenders agreed to write off £500m of borrowings in return for a 15pc stake in NCP.

As part of the deal, Macquarie was forced to inject £50m of fresh capital into the business. NCP’s biggest landlord, Israeli investment group Delek, also agreed to an annual rent bill reduction of £9m down to £40m.

It was only a temporary reprieve. When Macquarie sold NCP in 2017 to Park24, its executive Martin Stanley said: “We have worked to ensure that NCP is in the best possible position for continued growth.”

Yet just months later, Jo Cooper, the NCP chief executive, admitted that the company suffered from an image problem. Using its car parks was a “grudge purchase”, she told The Telegraph. “We’re second only to the tax man in terms of the ways people want to part with their money,” Cooper half-joked.

By 2021, despite borrowing £100m from shareholders that year, Park24 was in arrears on its rents and threatening to pull the plug on the business unless landlords agreed to a reduction and wrote-off half of what they were owed.

Pandemic lockdown restrictions had triggered a 40pc plunge in turnover in 2020, leaving it £257m in the red. Meanwhile, the company’s annual rent bill had long since ballooned past the £100m mark. “Without a fundamental shift in arrangements with landlords, there can be no certainty that NCP will survive,” the operator warned.

A costly restructuring deal was eventually struck that reduced its rent obligations but not by enough. Rental payments remained “the largest element of cash outflow”, it said, and the company was forced to borrow a further £180m from its parent company.

Still, it remained heavily loss-making, racking up a further £91m of losses that year, followed by £22.4m in 2022.

In 2023, NCP reported a slight uptick in turnover of £186.5m from £173m the previous year. The company’s “pricing and operational strategies” had led to an increase in customer numbers, finance chief Hideyuki Nagahiro said. Yet revenue was still 20pc lower than the year before Covid, and NCP finished the year £28.2m in the red.

By the end of 2025, total losses since the pandemic stood at more than £400m, debts had spiralled to £350m and it was sitting on 350 car parks, compared with an estimated 950 at its height.

Another round of rent payments due at the end of this month proved to be the nail in the coffin. “Demand has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations,” leaving the company with “insufficient cash available to meet its financial obligations,” PwC said. It will now seek buyers for all or parts of the business, with parking sites to remain open for now.

There was no mention of the part NCP’s reputation for eye-watering charges played in its downfall. The company has also faced criticism for levying punitive fines.

Last February, NCP apologised and quashed a string of incorrectly issued fines after a grandfather was wrongly asked to pay a £100 penalty charge for a 14-minute stay in Darlington, County Durham. Signs at the car park stated parking was free for 90 minutes.

In the end, it was left to the internet to deliver its verdict on the reasons for NCP’s demise.

“Maybe if they weren’t so ridiculously expensive, people may be able to afford to park in there [sic] car parks,” one Facebook user said.

“Good riddance!” said another.

A spokesman for Macquarie said: “Macquarie has had no role in NCP’s ownership for nearly a decade. When we sold our stake in 2017, the business was stable, well‑capitalised and delivering consistent growth.” Cinven and 3i declined to comment.

data.title: Married At First Sight star dies after cancer battle
data.description:
Married At First Sight’s Mel Schilling has died at the age of 54, days after revealing her colon cancer had spread to her brain.

Schilling, who was a relationship coach on the Channel 4 reality television series, was diagnosed with cancer in December 2023.

But in an Instagram post last week, the TV star said the disease had spread first to her lungs and then her brain.

In a statement published on Tuesday, her husband Gareth Brisbane said: “To most of you, she was Mel Schilling – matriarch of MAFS and queen of reality TV. To Maddie and me, she was our wee Melsie: an incredible mum, role model, and soulmate.

“On behalf of our family and her incredible friendship group, thank you for the support from around the world. Life can be beautiful, and life can be incredibly cruel. But ultimately, life is fleeting, fragile, and tomorrow is promised to no one.

“If you can do anything to honour Mel, please live life to the full, love your people well, and try not to sweat the small stuff. I had 15 wonderful years with my soulmate, and it was the privilege of my life to be by her side. For that, I will be forever thankful.

“Goodbye, my love. My one. Until we meet again.”

CPL Productions, which makes Married At First Sight UK, said it was “deeply saddened” by her death.

“Over many years, many of us have been privileged to work with Mel on Married At First Sight, and her loss today will come as a tremendous sadness to all who knew her,” the company said in a statement.

“Mel went far beyond being just a fantastic on-screen presenter; she was a friend to CPL and to many of us who worked with her.

“We send our love and heartfelt condolences at this incredibly difficult time to Mel’s family, friends, and all who were close to her. She will be deeply missed by everyone whose lives she touched.”

Channel 4 described her as “someone who radiated joy, warmth and optimism, who energised every room she walked into, with humour and positivity”.

In a statement, the station said: “All of us at Channel 4 are incredibly saddened by the news of Mel’s death. Our thoughts and condolences are, first and foremost, with her family and loved ones.

“We’re privileged to be the channel that is home to Mel’s work, which was at the heart of Married At First Sight’s phenomenal success, both in the UK and Australia. It reflected so much about her – her fierce advocacy for other women, her passion for healthy relationships and her mission to unite people in love.

“Just as importantly, we’d like to pay tribute to Mel as a person. For many who work for Channel 4, Mel was not just a colleague but a friend, someone who radiated joy, warmth and optimism, who energised every room she walked into, with humour and positivity.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Strip ex-Environment Agency chief of knighthood, say water campaigners
data.description:
Campaigners are calling for the former chief executive of the Environment Agency to be stripped of his knighthood, claiming he failed to combat sewage pollution while in post.

A petition calling for Sir James Bevan to be stripped of the honour has attracted almost 5,000 signatures since it was launched last week.

Sir James, who ran the Environment Agency from 2015 until March 2023, has been criticised for overseeing a reduction in water quality monitoring and accused of gagging staff from speaking out about the state of Britain’s rivers.

His track record came under scrutiny in the Channel 4 docuseries, Dirty Business, which aired earlier this month.

The series depicts the veteran mandarin as praising “operator self-monitoring”, meaning water companies were effectively allowed to mark their own homework on pollution rather than Environment Agency officers testing rivers.

Sir James downplayed concerns about pollution while at the helm of the Environment Agency. In 2023, he told a water industry conference that the debate around sewage pollution should be held “on the basis of facts not assertions”, adding: “There are some wild assertions, myths, and outright untruths flying around.”

The former Environment Agency chief also faced accusations of gagging staff. In an email to employees, Sir James said staff risked being sacked if they criticised or discredited the Environment Agency. Staff were even warned about commenting on the Environment Agency negatively inside work.

Southern Water was fined £90m during Sir James’s tenure after admitting to thousands of pollution incidents. It became clear that the Environment Agency had failed to identify that Southern Water was misreporting sewage discharges.

Carly Hardwidge, who set up the petition, said: “Revoking the knighthood from Sir James would send a powerful message. This action would restore the public’s faith in our honours system and reinforce the laudable aims of environmental accountability.”

Feargal Sharkey, the veteran water campaigner, backed the petition, saying: “I’d have to support the idea that he needs his knighthood removed because I can think of no reason why he should have ever earned it nor be allowed to retain it.”

‘Rewarding failure’

Since leaving the Environment Agency, Sir James has gone on to be a non-executive director at Welsh Water as well as a trustee of the Canal and Rivers Trust.

Earlier this week Welsh Water was hit with a £44.7m penalty following breaches in the supplier’s sewage network.

Henry Tuffnell, a Labour MP in Wales who sits on the Environment Committee, said: “Welsh Water dramatically needs to improve their environmental performance, investment and relationship with customers.”

Mr Tuffnell added: “It is therefore very concerning that they have rewarded failure in hiring Sir James Bevan, who has such a poor performance record from the EA.”

One of the key criticisms of the utilities industry is that weak executives move into the non-exec roles where they are ill-suited to challenging poor performance.

Alistair Carmichael, the head of the Environment Committee, said: “The problems of the water industry are ultimately problems of culture within the industry. Many of the problems come from poor corporate governance where under-performing executives are not properly challenged by regulators and their own non-exec directors.”

He added: “A revolving door where people can move easily from being poachers to gamekeepers serves only to make that problem worse. We will know if the Government is serious about changing the culture of the industry if, when they set up their new regulator, they take steps to lock that revolving door.”

A spokesman for Welsh Water said: “We appointed Sir James Bevan as a non-executive director in February 2025 following a rigorous search and appointment process.

“The Channel 4 programme is a television drama based on historical events relating to regulatory matters in England. Some dialogue, characters and scenes have been ‘recreated’ for the purposes of dramatisation. The portrayal of Sir James Bevan does not reflect the experience of the board since his appointment.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest because of his stake in a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is still listed as a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, co-founded the firm and is a minority shareholder.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, such as transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concern owing to his role at CCW, a government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the environment, food and rural affairs committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

After publication, Mr Wilson claimed that he had not undertaken any client advisory work for the firm for more than two years, and none at all since his appointment as CCW chairman. He said the website was outdated.

He also stressed that before that, he did not advise any clients in the public utilities sector. He denies that his stake in Political Insight gives rise to any conflict of interest regarding his role at the CCW.

The row will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 320-metre car journey.

As well as owning a stake in Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Royal Mail deserves appreciation for delivering post, says billionaire owner
data.description:
The billionaire owner of Royal Mail has told MPs that it deserves appreciation for delivering the post after it was described as “a national institution in meltdown”.

Czech tycoon Daniel Křetínský said Royal Mail was doing “a hard job that no one else in Europe is doing” and “the company and the people [Royal Mail employees] deserve appreciation for that”.

He pointed to postal services in other European countries where first-class post is three times more expensive than in the UK.

“If you decide to send a [first-class] letter from Brighton to the Scottish Highlands, you have to get it delivered the next day for £1.80,” he said. “It is not an easy job.”

Mr Křetínský’s defiant stance came as he was questioned by MPs on the Business and Select Trade Committee over mounting concerns about declining service levels.

Before Tuesday’s hearing, Liam Byrne, a Labour MP and the committee chairman, had called the Royal Mail “a national institution in meltdown”.

However, Mr Křetínský denied accusations that performance had deteriorated since his investment group EP bought the Royal Mail’s parent company, International Distribution Services, last year for £3.6bn.

The hearing was triggered by an investigation that found just 74.9pc of first-class post was delivered on time between April 2025 and January 2026 – way below a target of 93pc.

MPs found the discrepancy was equivalent to around 126 million first-class letters arriving late over the year.

With nearly 10pc of second-class letters also falling short of targets, it means a total of 219 million letters will arrive late this year, the committee has calculated.

Mr Křetínský acknowledged that Royal Mail was “not delivering on its promises” but refuted “the suggestion that service is declining”.

The billionaire businessman, who had opened his testimony by stating he was “a numbers person”, said he was “sorry for any letter that arrives late” but claimed “the numbers” don’t support claims of a drop in performance.

“Our quality of service, unfortunately, is relatively consistent over the last three years,” he added.

Asked by Mr Byrne whether he wanted “the committee to believe that service is currently adequate”, Mr Křetínský replied: “No, but there has been no decline in service. They are two different things.”

He also rebutted widespread claims that Royal Mail was routinely prioritising more lucrative parcel deliveries over letters, which are in decline.

In the previous session, trade union bosses had told the committee that workers were being instructed to leave NHS letters on racks and prioritise parcels instead.

“All employees want to deliver and some of them feel very aggrieved that they’re told to leave doctors’ and hospital letters in the frames to prioritise tracked [post],” Martin Walsh, deputy general secretary of the Communication Workers Union (CWU), said.

Mr Walsh said Royal Mail’s problems were being exacerbated by a “retention crisis” in which postal staff are “working harder than they’ve ever done in really challenging conditions, because they can’t clear the workload every day”.

Royal Mail bosses were also questioned about concerns that May’s local elections could be jeopardised by the late delivery of postal votes and election leaflets.

“Yes or no, are you guaranteeing to deliver postal votes to the country so that it can vote in elections on time?” Mr Byrne asked.

“We have put all planning in place so we are ready to deliver democracy for this country,” Alistair Cochrane, Royal Mail’s chief executive, said.

Mr Cochrane told the committee that Royal Mail is on course to deliver between 5.6 billion and 5.7 billion letters this year, compared with 6.3 billion last year and 20 billion in 2024.

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: AI boom threatened as Iran war triggers helium rationing
data.description:
The AI boom is under threat from the Iran war as helium rationing leads to a shortage of fibre-optic cables.

Supplies of helium, used in the production of the cables fitted in AI data centres, have been squeezed in recent weeks, by attacks on Gulf energy facilities.

Missile strikes on QatarEnergy’s Ras Laffan facility and a shipping shutdown in the Strait of Hormuz have hit supplies of liquid natural gas (LNG) production, which is used to make helium.

It has led to warnings of a global shortage of vital fibre-optic cables, which rely heavily on the gas during production.

Prysmian, the world’s biggest fibre-optic cable-maker, said there was a “risk to production across the sector,” while one of the world’s biggest helium suppliers, Air Liquide, also warned it was prioritising “critical applications” because of the shortfall.

Rob West, from research consultancy Thunder Said Energy, also said it was “likely to deprive global markets of helium that is crucial to the fibre optic production process”.

Helium is critical to the production of fibre-optic cables, used to rapidly cool molten glass as it is made into cables down which light signals travel.

While fibre-optic cables have typically been used for high-speed telecoms networks, increasing quantities are going into data centres used to power the AI boom, with demand doubling in 2024 and again in 2025.

Helium is also a critical input in the production of fibre-optic cables used in broadband and mobile networks, meaning any disruption can increase costs or even lead to shortages for telecoms operators.

The gas is also used for high-end semiconductor manufacturing and in medical applications such as MRI machines.

However, these are seen as more high-priority industries, meaning they are likely to face less disruption than areas such as fibre manufacturing.

Other uses such as welding and party balloons are also likely to face higher prices and shortages.

Semiconductor manufacturers have said they are monitoring the situation but do not expect an imminent shortage.

A spokesman for Air Liquide said: “As QatarEnergy declared force majeure on LNG production and associated products, there is a current industry-wide helium shortage globally.

“Our teams are fully mobilised to support our clients in this context, and we are taking actions such as prioritisation of critical applications. Air Liquide will allocate available supply from its storage cavern and various sources in different continents.”

A Prysmian spokesman said: “It will very likely increase prices, and it is a risk to production across the sector.

“The supply chain [is] somewhat geared towards high-margin uses, such as semiconductors in this moment.

“There is a real risk in fibre manufacturing because helium is directly used in optical fibre production, especially during the cooling phase when drawing and stabilising fibres.

“Fibre manufacturing – which uses lower total volumes but cannot operate without helium – risks slower deliveries and constrained availability.”

Helium spot prices have quadrupled since the outbreak of war in Iran.

Phil Kornbluth, of Kornbluth Helium Consulting, said that only a small amount was traded at the spot price, with most purchasers in long-term contracts.

However, he said both Air Liquide and Linde, another major supplier, had raised prices by introducing surcharges on contracts. Linde did not comment.

Telecoms industry sources insisted that there had not yet been any impact on the broadband rollout.

It comes as many smaller broadband companies scale back the rollout of their full-fibre networks as they struggle under huge debt costs, while operators have also built up their supplies of fibre optic cables following supply chain shocks sparked by the pandemic.

data.title: ‘My father-in-law died, then NS&I locked us out of his £50k savings’
data.description:
Have you been affected by NS&I delaying or withholding payouts? Email us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Bereaved families are considering court action after years of “stressful” dealings with National Savings and Investments (NS&I), which they say lost track of their loved ones’ savings after death.

Customers have described a total breakdown in communication with the provider and said they would pull their own money from accounts to avoid the same ordeal.

It comes as an investigation by The Telegraph found that taxpayers could be on the hook for a bill of up to £400m to return missing funds to families.

Retired nurse Wendy Maxted, from Ramsgate, Kent, spent months chasing NS&I for updates on her late father-in-law’s £50,000 premium bonds savings.

Ms Maxted, 64, who has been processing the loss of her mother and the pressure of having to retire early because of ill health, said the stress became unbearable.

When her father-in-law died last March, Ms Maxted took the lead on the paperwork and administration. But she didn’t expect that it would take a year in total to withdraw the funds.

She said: “It was stressful enough, but this just added so much more stress. I tried to phone, I did an online form – I never got any response.”

Eventually, Ms Maxted’s mother-in-law received a letter informing her that because of the amount held, a grant of probate would be required to release the funds.

Five months passed between the family getting probate and the money finally being released by NS&I, according to Ms Maxted.

The solicitor dealing with the matter visited Wendy’s mother-in-law at home as she was so frail.

“On top of losing her husband, she’s faced a battle to get his money out,” Ms Maxted said.

“My mother-in-law is withdrawing her own £50,000 in [premium bonds] because of the experience... She doesn’t want to put her sons through the same situation.”

Ms Maxted said it cost her family £1,800 in legal fees to access her father-in-law’s savings.

“It just seems so wrong,” she said.

Dozens of bereaved family members have contacted The Telegraph describing similar struggles. The government-backed bank has been accused of “short-changing” them as they have struggled to access cash that is rightfully theirs.

NS&I holds some £240bn for more than 24 million savers, making it one of Britain’s biggest banks.

But it has long said that a grant of probate or a letter of administration may be needed for anyone deceased with savings or premium bonds in excess of £5,000.

‘It’s been a horror story’

Graham Street, from Preston, described his own dealings with NS&I as a “horror story”. In a tearful conversation, he said he had faced sleepless nights with letters from NS&I and lawyers spread out around him.

His parents lived in Spain for most of their adult lives. In June 2017, his mother purchased £10,000 of premium bonds with NS&I.

When she passed away in 2021, an administrative nightmare began. Passed between NS&I and Spanish notaries, Mr Street said he had received conflicting advice on how to handle the probate process.

To add insult to injury, Mr Street said NS&I sent two letters to his mother with news of £100 and £50 wins. These came after the firm was informed she had died.

“It was horrifying,” Mr Street said.

Years later, he still hasn’t been able to access the funds.

He said that communication had gone “downhill” in the past few years, with referrals to a “backroom team” that he was not able to contact, and promises of letters that did not arrive.

Mr Street promised his father in the month before his passing last January that he would “get the money back”.

“It was extremely upsetting,” he said. “I was making a promise I wasn’t sure I would be able to carry out.”

He is now considering court action.

The 160-year-old institution has already faced criticism for spending billions of pounds of taxpayers’ money on a stalled digital transformation.

The public accounts committee called it a “full-spectrum disaster” in a report last month.

‘NS&I sent a letter to someone who died 16 years ago’

While financial planner Phil Billingham does recommend NS&I products to his clients at Perceptive Planning, he questions whether the firm understands the difficulties bereaved families face.

Before his own mother and father went into care, they cleared out a lot of paperwork.

His father, who had invested about £30,000 in premium bonds, died in 2021. After his death, Mr Billingham had to trawl through his belongings to find a letter from NS&I with his father’s bondholder number on it to trace the money.

“I knew it was there, and I work in financial services. I knew how to find the money, but a lay-person wouldn’t have been able to do it.”

Mr Billingham said he also received two letters to his home with NS&I prizes addressed to deceased people.

“One of them has been dead for about 16 years, but still there is post turning up with winnings,” he said.

Mr Billingham suggested that NS&I’s “refusal” to deal with email slows processes down, creating further complications for grieving clients.

“Everything has to be posted, they think that is looking after taxpayers’ money,” he added, referring to the fact that the savings body is backed by the Treasury.

Simon Kent told The Telegraph that he informed NS&I of his mother’s death 25 days ago, with little response. She held about £1,400 in premium bonds.

“It’s not a large amount, it’s just the frustration more than anything,” he said.

Other readers told us about a lack of communication. One said he had been trying to use the NS&I tracing service to track down premium bonds for more than a year.

Another, called Chris, said that as executor for his mother, he was awarded probate in January. He said everything related to his mother’s estate has been tied up – apart from NS&I, where he is struggling to get it to close the account which holds £100. He said this was despite calling repeatedly and filing the necessary paperwork.

An NS&I spokesman said it recognised that dealing with bereavement could be “challenging”.

The firm also offered an apology “to anyone who has not received the customer service from NS&I that they should expect, particularly at such a sensitive time”.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Taxpayers foot bill to save £15m rail yard sunk by Reeves raid
data.description:
Labour is to spend taxpayers’ money on taking over a £15m rail-freight project abandoned by Eurotunnel after Rachel Reeves tripled the company’s business rates bill.

Network Rail is poised to step in to save a planned upgrade of the Barking Eurohub freight terminal in east London, with the project to be bankrolled with government money.

Eurotunnel walked away from the project and its other UK investments in November, when the Treasury revealed that its rates bill would jump from £22m to £65m.

The upgrade of the Barking Eurohub is viewed as vital to kickstarting cargo flows through the Channel Tunnel after only a handful of freight trains used the link last year.

The Department for Transport (DfT) said Network Rail, which is to be merged with nationalised train companies to form Great British Railways, would take control of the yard and its property development arm, Platform4. 

DfT described it as a “government-backed deal” for the Barking site, which is currently owned by Legal & General. 

Network Rail said it was in talks with potential future operators for the terminal, which could bring in £15m of investment, while “getting on with delivering the works required to reinvigorate the site and make it fit for purpose.”

A spokesman for Network Rail said the track and station operator would work to “transform the site into a cargo hub”.

Labour ‘driving away investment’

However, critics criticised the deal on Wednesday. Jerome Mayhew, the shadow rail minister, claimed that Network Rail was being forced to step in after the Chancellor’s “astronomical” business rates increase drove out Eurotunnel.

He said: “When business leaders and rail operators warned that these punitive increases would have real-world consequences, this is exactly what they meant.

“Labour’s own tax decisions have driven away investment and forced taxpayers to underwrite infrastructure that should have been delivered commercially.”

The DfT argued that the plan for the Barking terminal would revive the cross-Channel rail freight market and boost trade links with Europe, helping to stimulate the economy and reducing road congestion and emissions.

It said the key to the redevelopment would be to allow full-size European container trains restricted to the High Speed 1 route to reach London.

That would enable food markets to receive fruit and vegetables direct from overseas growers and open up the possibility of Scotch whisky and British consumer items being exported by rail.

Network Rail said it envisaged three trains a day running into the terminal from the Continent, connecting with services to Scotland via central England and the North West and removing 140,000 HGVs from the roads each year.

Maggie Simpson, the director general of the Rail Freight Group, said the Government’s intervention was necessary to “keep the dream of Channel Tunnel cargo alive” and safeguard a site whose loss would have prevented container trains from ever using the link.

However, she said that factors including unfair cost advantages that favour the trucking industry would need to be addressed if significant volumes of freight were to use the tunnel.

Ms Simpson said ministers were also compelled to step in because of the Barking yard’s importance to containerised defence-related shipments at a time of heightening international tension.

She said: “If you look at Britain’s strategic resilience it is a vital national asset for whatever you want to transport to and from Europe, and that includes defence shipments”.

The Barking site, originally developed by Deutsche Bahn’s DB Cargo for automotive shipments, received the first direct freight train from China to the UK in 2017, when a rake of 34 containers arrived there after an 18-day journey.

data.title: Are we on the brink of the next great financial crash?
data.description:
Many expats have been fleeing Dubai as fast as they decently can to escape the war with Iran, but there is at least one financier who has reportedly gone the other way.

This is Paresh Raja, the founder and, until last month, chief executive of a shadowy and now defunct London-based property lender called Market Financial Solutions (MFS).

MFS collapsed last month amid allegations of widespread fraud, prompting searching questions about how mainstream banks such as Barclays and Santander – with an exposure of nearly £1bn between them – could have become involved in such a high-risk operation.

Yet its demise is not just of interest to business-minded scrutineers of balance sheets. The wider issue is whether its collapse is a harbinger of much worse to come – whether the largely unregulated world of so-called “shadow banking” is now colliding with the economic ruin of renewed conflict in the Middle East to provoke, not quite 20 years after the last one, another Great Financial Crash?

A specialist provider of bridging loans and buy-to-let mortgages that operated in the opaque world of substantially unregulated finance, MFS had at least one claim to wider fame – the opulence and decor of its Mayfair headquarters.

Reflecting the personal tastes of Raja, these were heavily adorned with sports and entertainment memorabilia, including autographed cricket bats and signed copies of a number of Pink Floyd albums.

When insolvency practitioners were sent into the offices earlier this month, they were said to have found them largely stripped bare, with key pieces of memorabilia removed. Raja himself is believed to be in the Emirates, with a court order having imposed an asset freeze.

It’s not clear when Raja left the country or whether he is still in Dubai, where he has long held residency rights. His lawyers insist there is no question of him “fleeing” the country.

Administrators are meanwhile seeking to sell at least 250 high-end properties with links to MFS across some of London’s most exclusive neighbourhoods, including Mayfair, Belgravia, Kensington, Knightsbridge, Fitzrovia, Marylebone and Nine Elms.

A spokesman for Raja, Mike Stubbs of the solicitors Mishcon de Reya, told The Telegraph that “mistakes have been made, but there has been no intention to defraud whatsoever, and Mr Raja has not been the beneficiary of any shortfall (if any) there may be.” He further claimed that allegations of fraud were based on “a fundamental misunderstanding” and a number of “incorrect assumptions”.

The rise of the ‘shadow banking sector’

The whole affair has shone a bright – and, as far as the industry is concerned, unwelcome – light on the murky but fast-growing world of “private credit”, also referred to as the “shadow banking sector”.

It is vast. Largely unnoticed, global “private credit” assets have soared from around $4tn in 2008 to some $16tn today. Some estimates are substantially higher. It’s impossible to be certain, given that this is an area of lending which is substantially unmonitored.

And if you think the main consumer banking system is insulated from private credit’s wild west characteristics, think again. Analysis by the Bank of England suggests that UK banks alone have an exposure of around £173bn to private market funds and the companies they support.

In a recent interview, Lloyd Blankfein, former boss of Goldman Sachs, said he could “smell” a fresh financial crisis in the making and drew parallels between today’s set of circumstances and those leading up to the banking meltdown of 2008/9.

“I wonder where there’s hidden secret leverage,” he said of the growth in lending outside mainstream banking, in remarks that quickly went viral. “Now everyone says, ‘Oh, the world’s not leveraged’. That’s exactly what everybody said in the mortgage crisis until you suddenly discover that there was a lot of mortgage risk in Iceland.”

“It sort of smells like that kind of a moment again,” Mr Blankfein added. “I don’t feel the storm, but the horses are starting to whinny in the corral.”

The similarities between 2008 and today are indeed striking, and not just in the build-up of suspect credit. The other big similarity with the last financial crisis is energy prices. As then, the world economy is once more being hit by an oil-price shock. These events are nearly always recessionary, causing loans to turn bad that in good times are presumed to be safe.

In the run-up to the 2008 financial crisis, prices at the pumps soared, which in turn caused economic demand to plummet and loans to start defaulting. As it turned out, banks had nowhere near sufficient capital to withstand the consequent losses in their loan books.

The comparison with today is admittedly not an exact one. Back then, the rise in energy prices was mainly about excess demand, which had in turn been prompted by rapid industrialisation in China and other parts of the developing world. Just about every commodity surged in price, leading to a pronounced spike in overall inflation.

Today’s surge in energy costs has little to do with demand but is instead caused by interrupted supply through the Strait of Hormuz. Whether this makes it worse or less impactful than the 2008 shock is, at this stage, hard to tell. It depends on how long the Strait remains closed, with oil and gas prices elevated.

The process of bringing demand and supply back into balance is nevertheless bound to be painful. If supply remains restricted, demand will have to adjust accordingly, and that’s bound to have negative consequences for credit markets.

For now, the increase in oil and gas prices is not quite as big as back then, when the price of oil more than doubled. But with each passing day, the size of the shock grows larger. As is now abundantly clear, the White House badly underestimated the costs of the Iranian assault in terms of global energy prices and the world economy.

Central banks around the globe are already muttering the “s” word: stagflation, that deadly combination of high inflation and low or negative growth widely associated with the oil price shocks of the 1970s. In such circumstances, the ability to respond to recessionary forces with interest rate cuts is curtailed by rising inflation.

Already we are seeing signs of stress in credit markets, and particularly credit provision outside the main banking system.

The term “private credit” covers a multitude of different forms of finance, but broadly it applies to anything that lies beyond the reaches of mainstream banking supervision.

Its growth is in some respects a direct consequence of the last financial crisis: the regulatory crackdown on commercial, retail and investment banking that followed the implosion of 2008/9 has caused finance to seek less highly regulated environs elsewhere.

Thus does finance obey the waterbed principle of provision; press it down in one area and it merely rises up somewhere else. Tougher rules and capital requirements in mainstream banking have made the sector a lot safer than it was, but they have also pushed credit of more questionable quality out into the shadows, where it is hard to assess the dangers.

Of course, compared to mainstream bank and bond-market lending, private credit is still a relative tiddler, despite the growth of recent years. This has prompted many commentators and some regulators to dismiss it as systemically unimportant: If investors suffer losses in private credit, tough; it’s not going to bring the entire system down.

Or so goes their theory.

Parallels with the subprime lending crisis

It should be recalled, however, that much the same thing was said of subprime lending in the run-up to the collapse of Lehman Brothers. When HSBC revealed massive write-offs on its subprime US lending in early 2008, it looked unfortunate but didn’t seem fundamentally to undermine the entire bank.

Commentators conned themselves into believing the problem would remain relatively contained. In the event, it wasn’t. Many banks had loaded up with debt instruments called CDOs, posing a direct threat to solvency when it turned out these were close to worthless.

Today, optimists once again say the problem – if there is one – can be contained. But the early signs of trouble in private credit are by no means confined to relative outliers such as Paresh Raja’s MFS. A similar lack of transparency clouds the entire sector. Unlike mainstream banks, private-credit providers do not typically “mark-to-market” – that is, adjust the value of the loan book to reflect any deterioration in the quality of the credit. This makes it difficult to know what’s really going on in many loan portfolios.

Just lately, however, there have been a number of acknowledgements that all is not well. For instance, BlackRock recently slashed the value of a $25m loan to the e-commerce company Infinite Commerce Holdings to zero just three months after assessing it at full value. If this is anything to go by, credit quality is much worse than generally appreciated.

Investors are already being hit. In recent weeks there has also been a veritable explosion in private-credit “drawdowns”, where they are called on by lenders to provide additional capital to support loan books. But many investors are actually rushing to get their money out altogether, creating liquidity problems in many such funds.

“Gating”, where funds are effectively closed to withdrawals, has become commonplace. BlackRock’s $26bn HPS Corporate Lending Fund is just one such example, having refused nearly half of redemption requests for the first quarter, while one of the world’s largest “shadow banks”, Apollo Global Management, announced this week that it had blocked cash withdrawals after investors tried to pull $1.6bn in just three months.

Rules governing how much can be withdrawn and when are written into most private-credit contracts, but they have been only partially successful in preventing a wider stampede for the door.

Nobody would care much about the losses if they were confined to the likes of Marc Rowan, Apollo Global Management’s CEO, and other billionaires who have been gorging on private credit.

Have lending standards slipped again?

But perhaps inevitably, there is evidence of them spilling out into the wider financial system. When combined with other negative trends in financial markets, there is ample scope for the travails of private credit to transmogrify into a wider bust.

The fact that an institution as apparently respectable as Barclays Bank should have lent such large sums of money to MFS raises awkward questions about the wider quality of its loan book. Have standards slipped again? Credit follows a well-defined cycle; as the good times roll, bankers become ever more oblivious to risk, and, in the hunt for yield, end up lending to almost anyone with a story to tell, however unconvincing.

Many private-equity operators have, moreover, bought heavily into insurers. These insurers are then leant on to provide credit to companies in the wider portfolio, threatening to multiply the losses in any subsequent downturn.

Private credit has also been a big lender to software and other business services enterprises now at existential risk from the march of artificial intelligence. AI is in turn itself widely thought to be a bubble where rampant over-investment is likely to result in substantial eventual losses.

The result is that shares in some of the leading lights of private credit have suffered a hammering in recent months. These losses have been further compounded by the latest conflict in the Middle East, which is casting a wider pall over all stock market investment. Only the oil men are rubbing their hands in glee at the turn of events.

Serious financial crises tend to come around every 20 years or so, which is about as long as it takes for memories of the last one to fade and for regulators and practitioners alike to take their foot off the brakes. This would make us about due for one.

Furthermore, today’s maelstrom finds the world’s richer nations already up to their necks in sovereign debt and therefore unable to respond in any meaningful way with fiscal stimulus to halt further deterioration in the economy. Few are in a position to answer the current cost of living squeeze with the kind of energy-price support provided in 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine.

The humongous costs of Britain’s Energy Price Guarantee was very much part of the downfall of Liz Truss, UK prime minister at the time. Bond markets are even less likely to tolerate it this time around. Already gilt yields have surged by way of warning, dashing Government hopes of lower interest rates to support growth.

Predicting exactly when a financial market crash might happen is always going to be impossible. Who knows, we might again just about scrape through without one. But many of the preconditions are already there, and the way things are going in the Gulf, it won’t take much to tip us over the edge.

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: Disney abandons $1bn deal with OpenAI after it cancels ‘AI slop’ app
data.description:
Disney has cancelled a $1bn (£750m) investment in OpenAI after the tech giant killed its AI video app after just six months.

In an abrupt announcement, the company behind ChatGPT said it would shut down Sora, a service that allowed users to create hyper-realistic AI videos. The decision comes as OpenAI seeks to control its spiralling costs.

The move also sees the end of a $1bn licensing deal with Disney tied to the app, announced just three months ago.

“We’re saying goodbye to the Sora app,” OpenAI said. “To everyone who created with Sora, shared it, and built a community around it: thank you.”

A Disney spokesman said: “We respect OpenAI’s decision to exit the video generation business and to shift its priorities elsewhere.”

The launch of Sora last year was seen as a threat to the modern film industry and acting jobs. Its app also allowed users to use copyrighted characters from the likes of Disney and Nintendo in a casual way.

OpenAI eventually began blocking users from creating copyrighted characters following backlash from Hollywood. In December, it signed a $1bn investment deal with Disney, apparently drawing a line under the dispute.

Disney even said it would allow curated Sora clips on its Disney+ app.

But despite attracting more than 10 million downloads, Sora proved staggeringly expensive, requiring huge amounts of computing power to generate viral videos – dubbed by critics as “AI slop”.

According to one estimate in Forbes, it may have cost as much as $15m per day in computing power alone. Billy Peebles, the head of Sora, said last year: “The economics are currently completely unsustainable.”

It comes as Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, scrambles to put a lid on soaring costs as he seeks to list the company later this year at a valuation of up to $1tn.

OpenAI has been racing to shore up its finances after pledging to spend as much as $600bn on AI data centres by 2030.

Last month, it announced it had raised $100bn from investors, including Amazon and Nvidia. Yet it has projected it will lose $14bn this year alone.

Despite enjoying huge success with consumers through its ChatGPT app, Mr Altman has been seeking to trim costly side projects and focus on more lucrative business customers.

Anthropic, which develops the rival Claude AI app, has enjoyed glowing reviews from businesses. The rival AI lab has largely avoided launching flashy products aimed solely at consumers and eschewed video and image generation entirely.
