data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s high streets ‘dragged down by vape shops’
data.description:
Britain’s high streets are being dragged down by a proliferation of vape shops and empty stores, the Communities Secretary has said.

Steve Reed said the decline of town centres had eroded pride in local areas as he vowed that the Government would bring “high streets back to life” by reopening shops and supporting businesses.

It comes as ministers unveiled a £301m package for councils to spend on revitalising town centres by investing in local businesses, building new homes, libraries and parks.

‘A kind of quiet patriotism’

Speaking to The Telegraph, Mr Reed said: “For most people, what they see when they step outside their front door sets the tone for their day and often their life.

“And when the town where you live feels run-down, it drags everything down.

“Half the shops shut and the rest struggling or selling vapes. A playground with broken swings nobody’s bothered to fix. A bus shelter with broken glass that has been like that for years.

“But you also see people who care deeply about where they live and deserve a government that cares too.

“This week we’re showing them we do, putting £301m bringing high streets back to life – reopening shops and backing local businesses.

“This goes far beyond just how places look. It’s about whether people feel they belong, can take pride in where they live, and see a decent future for themselves and their family. 

“As Sunday Telegraph readers will recognise, that pride is a kind of classic patriotism: quiet, unshowy, rooted in wanting your town to be somewhere you want to call home.”

Mr Reed said a further £18m had been allocated to restore and update children’s playgrounds in the most deprived parts of Britain.

The investment is part of the Government’s Pride in Place initiative, which ministers hope will arrest Labour’s slide in the polls ahead of the local elections on May 7.

The party has slumped to third in the latest YouGov weekly voting intention polls on 17 per cent, two points behind the Greens and eight points behind Reform UK.

Mr Reed has vowed to target vape shops and US-style sweet shops in his role as Communities Secretary.

He previously said people were “sick to death” of seeing those shops taking over high streets, some of which “turn out to be fronts for organised crime”.

His comments come after a study warned that Labour would be “washed away in a tide of discontent” at the next general election unless it tackled the decline of high streets.

Research by the University of Southampton found that people felt high streets had declined more than any other part of their local area over the past decade as well-known retailers collapsed and shoplifting rose.

Last week, The Telegraph revealed that the Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s business rates increase next month will see high street convenience shops face an average rise of £1,600 over the next two years.

Sir Mel Stride, shadow chancellor, said Ms Reeves’s actions risked undermining Britain as a “nation of shopkeepers”.

This is about restoring pride in where you live

By Steve Reed

For most people, what they see when they step outside their front door sets the tone for their day and often their life.

And when the town where you live feels run-down, it drags everything down.

Half the shops shut and the rest struggling or selling vapes. A playground with broken swings nobody’s bothered to fix. A bus shelter with broken glass that has been like that for years.

But you also see people who care deeply about where they live and deserve a government that cares too.

This week we’re showing them we do, putting £301m bringing high streets back to life – reopening shops and backing local businesses.

This goes far beyond just how places look. It’s about whether people feel they belong, can take pride in where they live, and see a decent future for themselves and their family. 

‘Fixes for things that matter’

As Sunday Telegraph readers will recognise, that pride is a kind of classic patriotism: quiet, unshowy, rooted in wanting your town to be somewhere you want to call home.

Places matter. They always have. But for too long successive Tory governments let them to go to the wall.

So, we’re investing £18m to repair and replace playgrounds in 66 deprived communities, from Tyneside to Torquay.

We’re also backing the first Pride in Place projects with up to £20m over a decade. That’s £5.8bn going straight into 284 communities across the UK, with initial investment plans already approved and ready to deliver. Money for neighbourhoods that need it most, whose residents will decide how it’s spent.

These aren’t trophy schemes or glossy buildings – they’re fixes for the things that really matter and make everyday lives better.

Some people like to say nothing can change and the system is beyond repair.

That’s not patriotism. It’s giving up while pretending to tell hard truths.

Real patriotism – the kind I believe in – means backing the country as it actually is: in the streets, parks and town centres people use every day.

It’s about reversing decades of neglect in places promised the world and given nothing. “Levelling up”. “Taking back control”. The slogans came and went. The broken swings stayed broken.

The money we’re putting into playgrounds matters because no child should miss out on the joy of playing outdoors with their friends.

‘It’s about people power’

In the 21st century, town centres thrive when they have a proper mix of life – homes, health centres, libraries, community hubs and green spaces alongside shops.

As part of our High Streets Strategy, we are investing in making our high streets fit for the future and we’ll confirm the first locations in the coming weeks.

For us, it’s about whether places feel looked after and right for their residents. Can you buy essentials on your doorstep? Do you have any real power to improve your neighbourhood – to take on the fly-tipping, the graffiti, that broken pavement?

This is what our £5.8 billion Pride in Place programme is all about – people power. And I’m delighted to see the exciting first projects we’ve just confirmed are going ahead.

In Spennymoor, Co Durham, they’re setting up a young people’s fund. In Dewsbury, West Yorkshire they’re creating a prevention team to tackle crime and anti-social behaviour.

In Greenock, Scotland a new Enterprise Hub will support local start-up businesses. That’s change in action.

For too long, decisions were taken at a distance and services built around bureaucracy. The era of the top-down state must end. People want – and deserve – a real say in how their schools, surgeries and job centres work.

Money and decisions pushed closest to the people who know what needs changing always go further.

And this is only the beginning.

The new generation of new towns we’ve committed to building will mean more homes, more affordable homes, more jobs, better transport and they’ll be greener – a turning point in how we create thriving communities.

We’ll be saying more about this very soon – that’s the next chapter.

Street by street, neighbourhood by neighbourhood, we’re backing people with the power to shape their own futures.

Steve Reed is the MP for Streatham and Croydon North and the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘It’s a shame’: US mustard maker in talks to buy Colman’s and Marmite
data.description:
The US maker of French’s yellow mustard is in talks to buy Marmite and Colman’s, making them the latest British brands that could fall into American hands.

Unilever, which owns the spread and sauce, said it had received an offer for its food division from Baltimore-based spice and sauce maker McCormick.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from the Bass brewery. Colman’s was founded in Norwich in 1814 and is still made from mustard seeds grown in the area.

The food industry reacted with dismay to news of the potential sale, following other US takeovers of historic brands such as Cadbury and Lea & Perrins.

John Farrand, the managing director of the Guild of Fine Food, called the potential sale “sad because that’s removing revenue and business from the UK”.

He said: “These producers get amalgamated into big venture capital funds, and once the accountants get hold of them, they become a number on a spreadsheet.”

Jim Winship, of the British Sandwich and Food to Go Association, said: “It’s a shame, in a way, that we lose our brands, but we live in a global world where these things happen all the time.

“We’re not very good at keeping innovations, so perhaps we’re a bit remiss as a country for not protecting our own.”

News of the offer follows weeks of speculation that Unilever had been in discussions with Kraft Heinz to combine their food operations.

In a statement posted on Friday, the company said it believed that its food division, which also includes Hellmann’s Mayonnaise and Knorr stock cubes, remained a “highly attractive business, with a strong financial profile led by market-leading brands in growing categories”.

It said it was “confident in the future of the goods business as part of Unilever”. However, it said it had received an offer from McCormick & Co and was currently in talks with the US giant.

As well as French’s, a sweet mustard typically associated with hot dogs, McCormick also makes Frank’s Red Hot and Cholula hot sauces and various spice blends, including Old Bay seasoning.

Unilever said there was “no certainty any transaction will be agreed”. Its food business is estimated to be worth roughly $36bn (£27bn). A sale would leave the company focused on home and beauty brands such as Dove, Vaseline and Lynx.

Food shake-up

Unilever recently demerged its ice cream business, spinning it out as The Magnum Ice Cream Company.

The new entity, which was the result of an almost two-year-long process, is now home to Ben & Jerry’s and Cornetto, as well as the eponymous ice cream brand.

Unilever has been on a major drive to trim down its sprawling portfolio of food brands.

It sold its spreads business in 2017, which included I Can’t Believe it’s not Butter and Flora, and in 2022, it offloaded most of its tea business, including Lipton and PG Tips.

Fernando Fernandez, the chief executive, who took the reins of the company in March last year, recently said the company would continue to carve off brands.

In Unilever’s most recent annual report, Mr Fernandez outlined a vision for the company to achieve 65pc of its sales from beauty, wellness and personal care.

He told a conference in New York earlier this year: “We are really shifting our portfolio into more beauty, more wellbeing, more personal care.”

Unilever’s share price rose slightly following the announcement. However, analysts at Barclays said speculation over the future of the company’s food business was unhelpful.

In a note published on Friday, analysts said: “Unilever has been very clear that its future lies in beauty and wellbeing and not in foods. Exiting ice cream was about doing fewer things better with more impact.”

However, they said management had appeared to indicate at its last financial results that “an exit from foods wasn’t something on the table in the short term and even when it might be, it would need to be a compelling and value accretive option”.

The note by analysts at Barclays added: “Unlike previous CEO Hein Schumacher, who would have probably been more precise about the future of foods, it has always seemed that Mr Fernandez was more open-minded and less inclined to close down speculation about the long term future of its foods business.”
data.author: The telegraph
